contentBlocks.title: When do you need to convert Mbps to MB/s?
contentBlocks.html:
Internet speeds are advertised in megabits per second (Mbps), but file sizes and download speeds are typically shown in megabytes per second (MB/s). 1 byte = 8 bits, so 1 MB/s = 8 Mbps.

This means a "100 Mbps" connection downloads at a maximum of 12.5 MB/s. A "1 Gbps" connection = 125 MB/s.

This conversion is essential for estimating download times, choosing an internet plan, and understanding the difference between advertised and actual speeds.

All calculations run locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server.

items.title: 1. Enter a value
items.description: Type a number in the input field. You can use a period or comma as the decimal separator.

items.title: 2. Read the result
items.description: The conversion result appears instantly in the field next to it – no clicking required.

items.title: 3. Copy or reverse
items.description: Click Copy result or use the Reverse button to convert in the opposite direction.

items.title: ISP Plan Comparison
items.description: Comcast Xfinity tops at 1,200 Mbps (150 MB/s). AT&T Fiber offers symmetrical 5 Gbps (625 MB/s). Convert any advertised plan to actual MB/s to compare offers fairly.

items.title: Streaming Requirements
items.description: Netflix 4K requires 25 Mbps (3.1 MB/s). YouTube 4K needs 20 Mbps (2.5 MB/s). A household running three 4K streams simultaneously needs at least 75 Mbps (9.4 MB/s) dedicated to streaming.

items.title: Remote Work Bandwidth
items.description: Zoom HD video needs 3.8 Mbps upload (0.47 MB/s). AWS S3 uploads at 500 Mbps (62.5 MB/s) theoretical max. FTP upload to a VPS on 100 Mbps uplink maxes out at 12.5 MB/s.

items.title: VPN and Transfer Overhead
items.description: VPNs typically add 10–15% overhead due to encryption. A 100 Mbps connection through a VPN delivers roughly 85–90 Mbps effective throughput — about 10.6–11.25 MB/s instead of 12.5 MB/s.

items.title: Complete privacy
items.description: All calculations run locally in your browser. No data is sent to any server.

items.title: Real-time results
items.description: The result updates instantly as you type – no need to click any button.

items.title: Bidirectional conversion
items.description: The Reverse button lets you convert in both directions with one click.

items.title: Reference table
items.description: A ready-made table with commonly converted values and contextual descriptions.

contentBlocks.title: How does Mbps to MB/s conversion work?
contentBlocks.html:
1 byte = 8 bits. Therefore: Mbps ÷ 8 = MB/s, and MB/s × 8 = Mbps.

Example: 200 Mbps ÷ 8 = 25 MB/s. This is the theoretical maximum download speed.

Actual speeds are typically 70–90% of the advertised maximum due to protocol overhead, network congestion, and other factors.

contentBlocks.title: Practical tips
contentBlocks.html:
• 50 Mbps = 6.25 MB/s. Good for streaming, browsing, video calls.
  • 100 Mbps = 12.5 MB/s. Good for multiple devices, 4K streaming.
  • 500 Mbps = 62.5 MB/s. Good for large downloads, gaming.
  • 1 Gbps = 125 MB/s. 1 GB downloads in ~8 seconds.

items.question: How many MB/s is 100 Mbps?
items.answer: 100 Mbps = 12.5 MB/s. Divide by 8 because 1 byte = 8 bits. Most US ISP plans (Comcast 100 Mbps tier, AT&T Basic Fiber) give you exactly this theoretical maximum.

items.question: Why is my download slower than advertised?
items.answer: ISPs advertise in Mbps (bits), but your download manager shows MB/s (bytes). 100 Mbps = max 12.5 MB/s. Real-world speed is typically 70–90% of that due to protocol overhead, Wi-Fi interference, and server limitations.

items.question: How long to download 1 GB at 100 Mbps?
items.answer: 1 GB = 1,000 MB. At 12.5 MB/s (100 Mbps): 1,000 ÷ 12.5 = 80 seconds theoretically. Realistically 90–120 seconds with overhead.

items.question: What speed do I need for 4K streaming?
items.answer: Netflix 4K Ultra HD requires 25 Mbps (3.1 MB/s). YouTube 4K needs 20 Mbps (2.5 MB/s). Disney+ 4K requires 25 Mbps. For a household with two 4K streams, plan for at least 50 Mbps.

items.question: How fast is Comcast Xfinity's top plan?
items.answer: Comcast's top residential plan is 1,200 Mbps (1.2 Gbps) download = 150 MB/s. Upload is typically 35 Mbps (4.4 MB/s) on cable. AT&T Fiber 5 Gbps is symmetrical — 625 MB/s both up and down.

items.question: How much bandwidth does a VPN use?
items.answer: A VPN adds 10–15% overhead for encryption (WireGuard) or up to 20% for older OpenVPN. On a 100 Mbps connection you can expect 85–90 Mbps effective throughput — about 10.6–11.3 MB/s instead of 12.5 MB/s.

items.question: What Mbps do I need for uploading to AWS S3?
items.answer: AWS S3 multipart upload with a 100 Mbps uplink achieves roughly 10–12 MB/s in practice. For large dataset transfers (100 GB+), consider AWS Direct Connect or Snowball for speeds above your ISP limit.

items.question: Are calculations done locally?
items.answer: Yes. All calculations run in your browser. No data is sent to any server.
